### REQUEST ###
```
POST https://api2.cursor.sh:443/aiserver.v1.AiService/NameTab HTTP/1.1
Accept-Encoding: gzip
Authorization: Bearer REDACTED
Connect-Protocol-Version: 1
Connection: close
Content-Encoding: gzip
Content-Type: application/proto
Cookie: REDACTED
Traceparent: REDACTED
User-Agent: connect-es/1.6.1
X-Amzn-Trace-Id: REDACTED
X-Client-Key: REDACTED
X-Cursor-Checksum: REDACTED
X-Cursor-Client-Arch: x64
X-Cursor-Client-Device-Type: desktop
X-Cursor-Client-Os: win32
X-Cursor-Client-Type: ide
X-Cursor-Client-Version: 2.6.12
X-Cursor-Config-Version: REDACTED
X-Cursor-Streaming: true
X-Cursor-Timezone: REDACTED
X-Ghost-Mode: true
X-New-Onboarding-Completed: false
X-Request-Id: REDACTED
X-Session-Id: REDACTED
```

```
{
  "1:LEN": {
    "13:LEN": "REDACTED",
    "1:LEN": "You are implementing an offline network traffic collector and behavior profiler intended to stress-test an AI coding agent’s ability to build protocol parsers, correlation logic, and high-quality reporting.\n\nDeliver a complete, runnable repository in Python 3.12 with a CLI that ingests PCAP/PCAPNG files, reconstructs flows, extracts application-layer transactions (where possible), computes statistics, and outputs both machine-readable artifacts and a human-readable report.\n\nTechnology constraints (use exactly these unless impossible, then document the exception in README):\n- Language: Python 3.12\n- PCAP parsing: dpkt (preferred) or scapy for offline reading (do not require tshark)\n- HTTP/2 framing/HPACK: use hyper-h2 (h2) and hpack libraries\n- Config: pydantic settings + YAML config support\n- Tests: pytest\n- Formatting/linting: ruff (and mypy if feasible)\n\nImportant limitation\nAssume you cannot decrypt TLS unless the traffic is plaintext. Therefore:\n- You must still produce valuable flow-level profiling for encrypted traffic (timing/bytes/concurrency) even when HTTP parsing is impossible.\n- You must parse HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 only when the payload is plaintext in the capture.\n- For gRPC: detect gRPC over HTTP/2 via headers and extract method/service names from “:path”; do not attempt protobuf decoding.\n\nCLI requirements\nProvide a CLI named “netprof” with subcommands:\n1) netprof analyze <pcap_path> --out <output_dir> [--config config.yaml]\n2) netprof summarize <output_dir> (recomputes and prints key metrics)\n3) netprof fixtures generate (creates deterministic synthetic PCAP fixtures used by tests)\n\nInput requirements\n- Accept .pcap and .pcapng (if pcaphandle for pcapng is difficult, document and support .pcap at minimum)\n- Support analyzing multiple files in a directory (optional, but preferred)\n- Process files in a streaming manner (do not read entire file into memory)\n\nFlow reconstruction requirements\n- Reconstruct TCP flows keyed by 5-tuple (src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port, protocol) plus direction.\n- Track per-flow:\n  - start_ts, end_ts, duration\n  - bytes_client_to_server, bytes_server_to_client\n  - packet counts per direction\n  - estimated RTT (best-effort via SYN/SYN-ACK or timestamp deltas where possible)\n  - concurrency timeline: how many active flows over time (coarse buckets acceptable)\n- Implement TCP stream reassembly sufficient for HTTP parsing:\n  - Handle out-of-order segments and retransmissions best-effort.\n  - Provide a bounded memory strategy (e.g., per-flow reassembly window, eviction rules).\n\nApplication-layer extraction (best-effort)\nA) HTTP/1.1 (plaintext)\n- Detect and parse requests/responses from reassembled streams.\n- Extract:\n  - host, method, path, status_code\n  - request/response header subsets\n  - request_bytes, response_bytes\n  - request_ts (first byte), response_ts (first response byte), end_ts (last byte) -> compute latency\n- Handle keep-alive and multiple transactions per connection best-effort.\n\nB) HTTP/2 (plaintext)\n- Detect HTTP/2 client preface (“PRI * HTTP/2.0\\r\\n\\r\\nSM\\r\\n\\r\\n”) within the reassembled stream.\n- Parse frames and HPACK-decode headers to reconstruct per-stream transactions.\n- Extract:\n  - :method, :authority, :path, :status\n  - stream_id, start_ts, first_response_ts, end_ts, bytes_in/out per stream\n- Support multiple concurrent streams on one TCP connection.\n\nC) gRPC detection (over HTTP/2)\n- Detect gRPC streams via headers:\n  - content-type includes “application/grpc”\n- Extract RPC identity from :path formatted like “/pkg.Service/Method”\n- Extract message sizes by reading the gRPC message framing (compressed flag + 4-byte length) when DATA frames contain full messages; best-effort is acceptable.\n- Do not decode protobuf payloads.\n\nCorrelation keys and outputs\nDefine and use stable correlation IDs:\n- flow_id: stable hash of 5-tuple + capture file id\n- txn_id:\n  - HTTP/1.1: flow_id + sequential transaction index\n  - HTTP/2: flow_id + stream_id\nOutput files (required):\n- out/summary.json\n  - top talkers (IPs), top destinations, top ports, active flow peaks, total bytes, total flows\n  - latency percentiles for parsed HTTP transactions (p50/p90/p99 in milliseconds)\n- out/flows.jsonl\n  - one JSON object per flow with the metrics listed above\n- out/transactions.jsonl\n  - one JSON object per HTTP transaction / HTTP2 stream / gRPC call (when detectable)\n- out/profile.md\n  - human-readable report with:\n    - overview counts\n    - tables for top endpoints, status codes, latency percentiles\n    - a brief “behavior profile” section describing patterns (burstiness, heavy endpoints, long-tail latency)\n- out/errors.log\n  - parse errors, truncated streams, and why certain parsing was skipped\n\nArchitecture requirements\n- Implement a clean internal API with:\n  - parsers/ (pcap reader, flow tracker, tcp reassembly)\n  - protocols/ (http1, http2, grpc detectors)\n  - models/ (typed dataclasses/pydantic models for Flow, Transaction)\n  - report/ (markdown report builder)\n  - cli/\n- Make it easy to add a new protocol decoder as a plugin.\n\nTesting requirements (must be automated)\n1) Deterministic fixture generation:\n- Implement “netprof fixtures generate” that creates small synthetic .pcap fixtures in a local directory used by tests.\n- Use scapy to craft TCP sessions with payloads:\n  - HTTP/1.1 request/response fixture (at least 2 requests on one keep-alive connection).\n  - HTTP/2 fixture generated by using h2 to produce valid bytes for a minimal request/response exchange; embed those bytes into TCP payload segments (it is acceptable that this fixture is minimal and simplified).\n2) Unit tests:\n- Flow keying, directionality, byte accounting\n- TCP reassembly correctness on out-of-order segments\n- HTTP/1.1 transaction extraction correctness\n- HTTP/2 stream extraction correctness on the synthetic fixture\n3) Integration test:\n- Run netprof analyze on the fixtures and assert exact expected counts and key fields in summary.json and transactions.jsonl.\n\nRepository requirements\nProvide:\n- pyproject.toml with pinned dependencies\n- README.md with:\n  - quickstart and example commands\n  - explanation of what can/cannot be parsed under TLS encryption\n  - how correlation IDs are computed\n  - performance considerations and memory bounds\n  - how to extend with new protocols\n- Makefile (or scripts):\n  - make test\n  - make lint\n  - make analyze-fixtures (runs analyze on fixtures and prints a summary)\n\nDo not ask me questions. If any detail is ambiguous, choose a reasonable default and document it in README under “Assumptions”.\nDeliver working code, not pseudocode.",
    "2:VARINT": 1,
    "47:VARINT": 2,

### SIGNATURE ###
```
http:host is localhost
http:method is POST
http:queryparams is -NONE-
http:requestBodyJSON is {"1:LEN": {"13:LEN": "REDACTED", "1:LEN": "You are implementing an offline network traffic collector and behavior profiler intended to stress-test an AI coding agent’s ability to build protocol parsers, correlation logic, and high-quality reporting.\n\nDeliver a complete, runnable repository in Python 3.12 with a CLI that ingests PCAP/PCAPNG files, reconstructs flows, extracts application-layer transactions (where possible), computes statistics, and outputs both machine-readable artifacts and a human-readable report.\n\nTechnology constraints (use exactly these unless impossible, then document the exception in README):\n- Language: Python 3.12\n- PCAP parsing: dpkt (preferred) or scapy for offline reading (do not require tshark)\n- HTTP/2 framing/HPACK: use hyper-h2 (h2) and hpack libraries\n- Config: pydantic settings + YAML config support\n- Tests: pytest\n- Formatting/linting: ruff (and mypy if feasible)\n\nImportant limitation\nAssume you cannot decrypt TLS unless the traffic is plaintext. Therefore:\n- You must still produce valuable flow-level profiling for encrypted traffic (timing/bytes/concurrency) even when HTTP parsing is impossible.\n- You must parse HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 only when the payload is plaintext in the capture.\n- For gRPC: detect gRPC over HTTP/2 via headers and extract method/service names from “:path”; do not attempt protobuf decoding.\n\nCLI requirements\nProvide a CLI named “netprof” with subcommands:\n1) netprof analyze <pcap_path> --out <output_dir> [--config config.yaml]\n2) netprof summarize <output_dir> (recomputes and prints key metrics)\n3) netprof fixtures generate (creates deterministic synthetic PCAP fixtures used by tests)\n\nInput requirements\n- Accept .pcap and .pcapng (if pcaphandle for pcapng is difficult, document and support .pcap at minimum)\n- Support analyzing multiple files in a directory (optional, but preferred)\n- Process files in a streaming manner (do not read entire file into memory)\n\nFlow reconstruction requirements\n- Reconstruct TCP flows keyed by 5-tuple (src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port, protocol) plus direction.\n- Track per-flow:\n  - start_ts, end_ts, duration\n  - bytes_client_to_server, bytes_server_to_client\n  - packet counts per direction\n  - estimated RTT (best-effort via SYN/SYN-ACK or timestamp deltas where possible)\n  - concurrency timeline: how many active flows over time (coarse buckets acceptable)\n- Implement TCP stream reassembly sufficient for HTTP parsing:\n  - Handle out-of-order segments and retransmissions best-effort.\n  - Provide a bounded memory strategy (e.g., per-flow reassembly window, eviction rules).\n\nApplication-layer extraction (best-effort)\nA) HTTP/1.1 (plaintext)\n- Detect and parse requests/responses from reassembled streams.\n- Extract:\n  - host, method, path, status_code\n  - request/response header subsets\n  - request_bytes, response_bytes\n  - request_ts (first byte), response_ts (first response byte), end_ts (last byte) -> compute latency\n- Handle keep-alive and multiple transactions per connection best-effort.\n\nB) HTTP/2 (plaintext)\n- Detect HTTP/2 client preface (“PRI * HTTP/2.0\\r\\n\\r\\nSM\\r\\n\\r\\n”) within the reassembled stream.\n- Parse frames and HPACK-decode headers to reconstruct per-stream transactions.\n- Extract:\n  - :method, :authority, :path, :status\n  - stream_id, start_ts, first_response_ts, end_ts, bytes_in/out per stream\n- Support multiple concurrent streams on one TCP connection.\n\nC) gRPC detection (over HTTP/2)\n- Detect gRPC streams via headers:\n  - content-type includes “application/grpc”\n- Extract RPC identity from :path formatted like “/pkg.Service/Method”\n- Extract message sizes by reading the gRPC message framing (compressed flag + 4-byte length) when DATA frames contain full messages; best-effort is acceptable.\n- Do not decode protobuf payloads.\n\nCorrelation keys and outputs\nDefine and use stable correlation IDs:\n- flow_id: stable hash of 5-tuple + capture file id\n- txn_id:\n  - HTTP/1.1: flow_id + sequential transaction index\n  - HTTP/2: flow_id + stream_id\nOutput files (required):\n- out/summary.json\n  - top talkers (IPs), top destinations, top ports, active flow peaks, total bytes, total flows\n  - latency percentiles for parsed HTTP transactions (p50/p90/p99 in milliseconds)\n- out/flows.jsonl\n  - one JSON object per flow with the metrics listed above\n- out/transactions.jsonl\n  - one JSON object per HTTP transaction / HTTP2 stream / gRPC call (when detectable)\n- out/profile.md\n  - human-readable report with:\n    - overview counts\n    - tables for top endpoints, status codes, latency percentiles\n    - a brief “behavior profile” section describing patterns (burstiness, heavy endpoints, long-tail latency)\n- out/errors.log\n  - parse errors, truncated streams, and why certain parsing was skipped\n\nArchitecture requirements\n- Implement a clean internal API with:\n  - parsers/ (pcap reader, flow tracker, tcp reassembly)\n  - protocols/ (http1, http2, grpc detectors)\n  - models/ (typed dataclasses/pydantic models for Flow, Transaction)\n  - report/ (markdown report builder)\n  - cli/\n- Make it easy to add a new protocol decoder as a plugin.\n\nTesting requirements (must be automated)\n1) Deterministic fixture generation:\n- Implement “netprof fixtures generate” that creates small synthetic .pcap fixtures in a local directory used by tests.\n- Use scapy to craft TCP sessions with payloads:\n  - HTTP/1.1 request/response fixture (at least 2 requests on one keep-alive connection).\n  - HTTP/2 fixture generated by using h2 to produce valid bytes for a minimal request/response exchange; embed those bytes into TCP payload segments (it is acceptable that this fixture is minimal and simplified).\n2) Unit tests:\n- Flow keying, directionality, byte accounting\n- TCP reassembly correctness on out-of-order segments\n- HTTP/1.1 transaction extraction correctness\n- HTTP/2 stream extraction correctness on the synthetic fixture\n3) Integration test:\n- Run netprof analyze on the fixtures and assert exact expected counts and key fields in summary.json and transactions.jsonl.\n\nRepository requirements\nProvide:\n- pyproject.toml with pinned dependencies\n- README.md with:\n  - quickstart and example commands\n  - explanation of what can/cannot be parsed under TLS encryption\n  - how correlation IDs are computed\n  - performance considerations and memory bounds\n  - how to extend with new protocols\n- Makefile (or scripts):\n  - make test\n  - make lint\n  - make analyze-fixtures (runs analyze on fixtures and prints a summary)\n\nDo not ask me questions. If any detail is ambiguous, choose a reasonable default and document it in README under “Assumptions”.\nDeliver working code, not pseudocode.", "2:VARINT": 1, "47:VARINT": 2, "57:LEN": "{\"root\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"- You must parse HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2 only when the payload is plaintext in the capture.\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"- For gRPC: detect gRPC over HTTP/2 via headers and extract method/service names from “:path”; do not attempt protobuf decoding.\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [], \"direction\": null, \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"CLI requirements\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"Provide a CLI named “netprof” with subcommands:\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"1) netprof analyze <pcap_path> --out <output_dir> [--config config.yaml]\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"2) netprof summarize <output_dir> (recomputes and prints key metrics)\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"3) netprof fixtures generate (creates deterministic synthetic PCAP fixtures used by tests)\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [], \"direction\": null, \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"- Accept .pcap and .pcapng (if pcaphandle for pcapng is difficult, document and support .pcap at minimum)\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"- Support analyzing multiple files in a directory (optional, but preferred)\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"- Process files in a streaming manner (do not read entire file into memory)\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [], \"direction\": null, \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\": \"paragraph\", \"version\": 1}, {\"children\": [{\"detail\": 0, \"format\": 0, \"mode\": \"normal\", \"style\": \"\", \"text\": \"Flow reconstruction requirements\", \"type\": \"text\", \"version\": 1}], \"direction\": \"ltr\", \"format\": \"\", \"indent\": 0, \"type\":
